findings.locus: §2.2 Specialized agents and collaboration — 'no architectural mechanism may exist whose function depends on preventing it'
findings.claim: The prohibition on any mechanism 'whose function depends on preventing' agent-to-agent communication is a decision this ADR does not surface in its Decision statement (§2, which commits agents that 'collaborate on any route') and rides only in prose. It is stated as absolute — 'no architectural mechanism may exist' — and as a foreclosing commitment on all future designs. The deliberation record (Item 2, self-review LAA note) itself flags this exact clause as a 'conformance-shaped operationalization' rather than the ratified never-preclude commitment, which the ratified text was 'agent-to-agent communication is first-class and never precluded.' The absolute never-a-preventing-mechanism form is a broader smuggled commitment than the ratified never-preclude; §5.2 then re-states it as a hard constraint on all designs.

cited_authority.ref: Deliberation record, Item 2 ratified commitment: 'agent-to-agent communication is first-class and never precluded'; self-review note flags §2.2's 'no mechanism whose function depends on preventing it' as conformance-shaped operationalization of that commitment

claim_variants: §2 commits agents that 'collaborate on any route' and §2.2 states 'agent-to-agent communication is a first-class platform capability: no platform design may preclude it, and no architectural mechanism may exist whose function depends on preventing it.' The second clause is an absolute foreclosing commitment on all future designs, re-stated as a hard constraint in §5.2 ('no design may ... introduce a mechanism whose function depends on preventing it'). The deliberation record's ratified commitment is narrower — 'agent-to-agent communication is first-class and never precluded' — and the session's own self-review flags §2.2's 'no mechanism whose function depends on preventing it' as a 'conformance-shaped operationalization' of the ratified never-preclude commitment, not the ratified commitment itself. The absolute never-a-preventing-mechanism form is a broader smuggled commitment than the ratified never-preclude, and it is never surfaced as its own decision in the §2 statement.

§2.2 commits, in prose, an absolute foreclosure — 'no architectural mechanism may exist whose function depends on preventing it' — and §5.2 restates it as a hard constraint on all future designs ('or introduce a mechanism whose function depends on preventing it'). This is a strictly broader, harder-to-reverse commitment than the never-preclude posture the §2 Decision statement carries (agents that 'collaborate on any route'). An absolute never-a-preventing-mechanism rule forecloses design space across every downstream SDD/DDR for the platform's life, and rides only in §2.2/§5.2 prose — it is not surfaced as its own sub-decision in the §2 enumeration. The deliberation record's own self-review (Leg 3) flags §2.2's 'no mechanism whose function depends on preventing it' as a 'conformance-shaped operationalization' of the ratified never-preclude commitment, whose ratified text was narrower ('agent-to-agent communication is first-class and never precluded'). A foreclosing commitment of this reversibility cost, riding as an operationalizing gloss rather than a first-class Decision, is a scope defect: the Decision block is the contract's index, and this ruling is invisible to a reader who treats the enumeration as complete.

findings.locus: §2.6 Component taxonomy — 'the model-access gateway is of this class'
findings.claim: The record decides that a model-access gateway ('ai-gateway') is a substrate service and names it as an instance ('the model-access gateway is of this class (infrastructure facade — model access, no judgment of record)'). This is a component-existence and classification decision — asserting that a model-access gateway component exists and fixing its class — riding in the taxonomy prose. §2.6's own rule is that 'This record enumerates no agent roster ... Components are named above only where existing canon already names them.' No cited upstream canon (ADR-001, ADR-002, DDR-001, DDR-002, SDD-001, ADR-008) names a model-access gateway / ai-gateway component; SDD-001 §2.2 states 'no model client exists in this service' and names no such gateway. The record thus names a not-yet-canonical component while claiming to name only canon-named ones — a scope defect against its own stated rule.

cited_authority.ref: ADR-003 §2.6 self-imposed rule ('Components are named above only where existing canon already names them') in conflict with the same section naming 'the model-access gateway' as a class instance, with no upstream canon in the set naming that component

findings.locus: §2.3 Governance anchors to the graph — checkpoint-gate invariant self-consistency
findings.claim: §2.3 asserts checkpoint gates 'anchor to checkpoints in the run and the graph, not to positions in a call graph; no routing choice can bypass a gate.' The record supplies no mechanism internal to itself by which a route-agnostic gate is actually unbypassable — it names the property as an invariant and cites 'the ADR-008 family' generically. ADR-008 §6 enforces its controls 'at three-hat review at design time' and its runtime instances are gateway-mediated; but a direct agent-to-agent route (permitted by §2.2, with no permission machinery per §2.8) that performs no graph write reaches no gateway and no graph-anchored gate. The claim 'no routing choice can bypass a gate' is stated as a load-bearing governance guarantee but is unsubstantiated for the direct-route, no-graph-write interaction the same record makes first-class — the gate anchors to graph checkpoints, and an interaction touching no graph checkpoint has no gate to anchor to.

findings.locus: §2.7 coordination trajectory vs ADR-002 §2.6 write-authority anchoring
findings.claim: Attacked the §2.7 trajectory clause for the failure mode of a downstream record silently widening an upstream authority (the UPSTREAM-AUTHORITY rule). §2.7 explicitly routes future coordination-authority widenings 'through the upstream authority structure, never around it … as a named, additive authority assignment under the owning record's own amendment process,' naming run-lifecycle authorship beyond the AOE (ADR-002 §2.6) as the exemplar and stating the record 'neither blocks nor pre-empts them.' This does not rescue an upstream invariant by downstream qualifier — it defers to the upstream amendment process rather than carving one out. The clause held.

findings.locus: §2.6 recursion clause ('A sub-run is a run') vs §2.5 property 1 (durable ReasoningSession) and §2.7's own amendment rule
findings.claim: §2.6 states 'A sub-run is a run: the §2.5 anatomy invariants apply at every level' with 'the composing agent as orchestrator of its sub-run', and §2.5 property 1 requires 'the run's ReasoningSession' for every run. ADR-002 §2.6 assigns ReasoningSession lifecycle ownership solely to the AOE; DDR-002 §4 fixes ReasoningSession as AOE-owned with no sub-session or nested-session shape. A recursively-composed sub-run whose composing agent (not AOE) acts as orchestrator therefore requires either a ReasoningSession authored/owned by a non-AOE agent — contrary to ADR-002 §2.6 — or a session-nesting shape the schema does not carry. §2.7's own trajectory rule states that widening run-lifecycle authorship beyond the AOE 'arrives as a named, additive authority assignment under the owning record's own amendment process.' §2.6's recursion clause commits sub-runs-carry-the-ReasoningSession-invariant now, ahead of that assignment — a consequence the record does not declare and a position §2.7 says requires an upstream amendment first.

cited_authority.ref: ADR-003 §2.5 ('the run's ReasoningSession') + §2.6 ('A sub-run is a run ... with the composing agent as orchestrator') vs ADR-002 §2.6 ('AOE ... owns the ReasoningSession lifecycle only') and ADR-003 §2.7's own rule routing run-lifecycle-authorship widening through the owning record's amendment process

findings.locus: §2.3 Governance anchors to the graph — 'no routing choice can bypass a gate'
findings.claim: §2.3 asserts, as a load-bearing invariant, that checkpoint gates 'anchor to checkpoints in the run and the graph, not to positions in a call graph; no routing choice can bypass a gate.' §2.2 makes direct agent-to-agent communication first-class ('Direct interaction between agents is permitted on the same terms as orchestrator-mediated interaction') and §2.8 commits that 'opening a direct agent-to-agent route requires no ratification act; the §2.3 invariants are the entire control.' The record supplies no mechanism internal to itself by which a gate anchored to a graph checkpoint binds a direct interaction that touches no graph checkpoint. The ADR-008 controls it cites for gates are, per ADR-008 §6, 'enforced at three-hat review at design time' with runtime instances gateway-mediated — an interaction performing no graph write reaches neither. The universal claim 'no routing choice can bypass a gate' is stated but not substantiated for the direct-route, no-graph-touch interaction the same record makes first-class: a gate anchored to graph checkpoints has nothing to anchor to on such a route.

cited_authority.ref: ADR-003 §2.3 ('no routing choice can bypass a gate') unsubstantiated against §2.2 (first-class direct agent-to-agent interaction) + §2.8 ('opening a direct agent-to-agent route requires no ratification act; the §2.3 invariants are the entire control'); gates anchor to graph checkpoints, and a direct interaction touching no graph checkpoint reaches no gate

claim_variants: §2.3 asserts as a load-bearing invariant that checkpoint gates 'anchor to checkpoints in the run and the graph, not to positions in a call graph; no routing choice can bypass a gate.' §2.2 makes direct agent-to-agent communication first-class ('Direct interaction between agents is permitted on the same terms as orchestrator-mediated interaction'), and §2.8 commits that 'opening a direct agent-to-agent route requires no ratification act; the §2.3 invariants are the entire control.' The unbypassability guarantee is graph-anchored, yet the record supplies no mechanism — internal or cited — by which a gate anchored to a graph checkpoint binds a direct agent-to-agent interaction that touches no graph checkpoint. The ADR-008 controls cited for gates are, per ADR-008 §6, enforced at three-hat review at design time with runtime instances gateway-mediated; an interaction performing no graph write reaches neither the sole-owner gateway (ADR-002 §2.5) nor a graph-anchored gate. The universal claim 'no routing choice can bypass a gate' is stated but unsubstantiated for exactly the direct-route, no-graph-write interaction the same record makes first-class.

findings.locus: §4.3 Alternative C — transport-carried directive propagation (RBT-11 fold) vs the relocation mechanisms it claims
findings.claim: Attacked §4.3's rejection rationale for whether the directive-bridge fold is substantiated by standing canon or asserted. §4.3 claims the constraint authority relocates such that 'there is no envelope to protect because there is no envelope,' resting on §2.4's four mechanisms: graph-resident constraints (the Standards plane, DDR-001), gateway consultation (ADR-002 §2.5 sole-owner gateway), version-pinning at consultation (DDR-001's version-pinning model), and evidence linkage in the RG (ADR-001 §2.2). Each cited relocation mechanism exists and is cited correctly in the set — the sole-owner gateway, the version-pinning temporal model, and the capture invariant are all standing accepted canon. The rejection's core — that transport-resident authority is what the bridge's integrity machinery compensated for, and that authority no longer resides in transport — is carried by those upstream mechanisms rather than by argument alone; the residues route to named owning loci (correlation → orchestration/observability; rendering → per-agent designs under ADR-001 §2.3). The attack that the fold left an unhomed integrity concern failed.

findings.locus: §2.2 Specialized agents and collaboration — 'no architectural mechanism may exist whose function depends on preventing it'
findings.claim: §2.2 commits, in prose, an absolute foreclosure — 'no architectural mechanism may exist whose function depends on preventing it' — and §5.2 restates it as a hard constraint on all future designs ('or introduce a mechanism whose function depends on preventing it'). This is a broader, harder-to-reverse commitment than the never-preclude posture the §2 Decision statement carries (agents that 'collaborate on any route'). An absolute never-a-preventing-mechanism rule forecloses design space across every downstream SDD/DDR for the platform's life and is not surfaced in the Decision statement — it rides only in §2.2/§5.2 prose. A commitment of this reversibility cost (it can only be walked back by amending a platform principle) that constrains all future designs deserves to be stated as a first-class sub-decision in §2, where its foreclosing scope is visible, rather than as an operationalizing gloss in the collaboration section.

findings.locus: §2.4 Constraints from the graph — 'predicate-evaluation semantics are routed to the forthcoming constraint-validator service design (per DDR-002 §3's obligation-closure assignment)' vs §7 Cross-References DDR-003 entry
findings.claim: The body at §2.4 now reads '(per DDR-002 §3's obligation-closure assignment)', but §7 Cross-References still reads: 'DDR-003 (Feedback Loop Governance) — governance policy for the promotion path; the routing of the Condition predicate vocabulary (the predicate grammar for scope conditions) onward to the forthcoming constraint-validator service design.' DDR-003 §9 routes only the promotion-scoped 'Condition predicate vocabulary — the predicate grammar for scope conditions'; §2.4 governs enterprise standards/directives/policy binding agents from the Standards plane, a distinct and broader object whose obligation-closure assignment is DDR-002 §3's ('the obligation-closure logic ... closure logic + manifest consumption → constraint-validator SDD'). The §7 entry attributes to DDR-003 a routing scoped to promotion Conditions while the section it supports governs a different object; the load-bearing routing authority for §2.4's predicate evaluation is DDR-002 §3, not DDR-003's.

cited_authority.ref: ADR-003 §7 (DDR-003 entry routing the Condition predicate vocabulary) as the cited authority for §2.4's general enterprise-constraint predicate-evaluation routing, vs DDR-003 §9 (routed item scoped to promotion 'Condition predicate vocabulary') and DDR-002 §3 (obligation-closure / rule_definition evaluation assigned to constraint-validator SDD)

findings.authority_locus: DDR-002 §3 (obligation-closure logic + manifest consumption → constraint-validator SDD) vs DDR-003 §9 (Condition predicate vocabulary scoped to promotion scope-conditions)

findings.locus: §2.6 recursion clause ('A sub-run is a run: the §2.5 anatomy invariants apply at every level') vs §2.5 property 1 and ADR-002 §2.6 / DDR-002 §4
findings.claim: §2.6 states 'A sub-run is a run: the §2.5 anatomy invariants apply at every level,' with 'the composing agent as orchestrator of its sub-run,' and §2.5 property 1 requires for every run 'the run's ReasoningSession and its captured reasoning (ADR-001 §2.2; ADR-002 §2.6). No run is unrecorded.' ADR-002 §2.6 assigns 'the Agent Orchestration Engine (AOE) ... owns the ReasoningSession lifecycle only', and DDR-002 §4 fixes ReasoningSession as 'Lifecycle owned by AOE' with no sub-session or nested-session shape. A sub-run whose composing agent (not AOE) acts as orchestrator and must carry a ReasoningSession per §2.5 property 1 therefore requires either a ReasoningSession lifecycle owned by a non-AOE component (contrary to ADR-002 §2.6) or a session-nesting shape DDR-002 §4 does not carry. The design specifies a behavior — sub-runs carry the ReasoningSession invariant at every level — that would not work as written against the cited AOE-only lifecycle assignment and the schema's flat session shape.

cited_authority.ref: ADR-003 §2.5 property 1 ('the run's ReasoningSession') + §2.6 ('A sub-run is a run ... with the composing agent as orchestrator of its sub-run') vs ADR-002 §2.6 ('AOE ... owns the ReasoningSession lifecycle only') and DDR-002 §4 (ReasoningSession lifecycle owned by AOE, no nested-session shape)

findings.locus: §2.6 — orchestrator composition-decision capture routed to 'ADR-001 §2.4's per-stage provision'
findings.claim: §2.6 routes whether the orchestrator's composition decisions constitute captured reasoning to 'the forthcoming agent-orchestration-engine service design under ADR-001 §2.4's per-stage provision.' ADR-001 §2.4 is titled 'Lifecycle scope and design-maturity honesty' and provides that per-stage reasoning-capture mechanics are 'specified in the service and data designs as they are authored', with its object being the enterprise SDLC lifecycle stages ('from idea capture through solutioning to operational onboarding'). The orchestrator is an intra-run coordination component, not an SDLC lifecycle stage; the cited provision's stated scope is a different axis than the capture question §2.6 routes to it. The routing resolves the deferral intent but cites a provision whose scope does not directly cover the routed object.

cited_authority.ref: ADR-001 §2.4 ('Lifecycle scope' — per-stage capture mechanics deferred to service/data designs, scoped to enterprise SDLC lifecycle stages) vs ADR-003 §2.6 (orchestrator composition-decision capture routed there, an intra-run coordination component not a lifecycle stage)

findings.locus: §2.6 Component taxonomy — 'the model-access gateway (the forthcoming ai-gateway-service, §7) is of this class' + §5.1 + metadata Description
findings.claim: §2.6 fixes the class of a component the platform has not yet decided to build: 'the model-access gateway (the forthcoming ai-gateway-service, §7) is of this class (infrastructure facade — model access, no judgment of record).' §5.1 lists 'the model-access gateway design' among the designs this record unblocks. §2.6's own governing rule immediately follows: 'This record enumerates no agent roster ... Components are named above only where existing canon already names them.' No upstream record in the set commits a model-access gateway / ai-gateway-service as a platform component; it appears only as forthcoming in §7. Whether SOFIA has a distinct model-access gateway substrate service — versus folding model access into agent designs — is a component-posture decision that deserves its own decision record, or an upstream commitment to cite, before this ADR fixes it as a canonical taxonomy instance and predicates §5.1's unblocking claim on its existence. Committing a not-yet-decided component's existence and class at platform-principle altitude is a position taken ahead of the decision it depends on.

cited_authority.ref: ADR-003 §2.6 self-imposed rule ('Components are named above only where existing canon already names them') in conflict with the same section fixing the class of 'the model-access gateway (the forthcoming ai-gateway-service)', with no upstream record in the set establishing that component

findings.locus: §2.6 recursion clause ('A sub-run is a run: the §2.5 anatomy invariants apply at every level') vs §2.5 property 1 and §2.7's own amendment rule
findings.claim: §2.6 commits 'A sub-run is a run: the §2.5 anatomy invariants apply at every level' with 'the composing agent as orchestrator of its sub-run,' and §2.5 property 1 requires for every run 'the run's ReasoningSession.' §2.7's own trajectory rule states that widening run-lifecycle authorship beyond the AOE 'arrives as a named, additive authority assignment under the owning record's own amendment process.' The recursion clause commits, now and as a standing invariant at every composition level, a position that §2.7 declares requires an upstream amendment first: a sub-run orchestrated by a composing agent (not the AOE) that must carry a ReasoningSession is a non-AOE-owned run record — the very run-lifecycle-authorship widening §2.7 defers. This is a decision-bearing position taken ahead of the upstream resolution it depends on; whether a composing agent may own a sub-run's run-lifecycle record deserves its own decision (an upstream amendment or an explicit deferral) before this record binds it as an every-level invariant. That the record commits it here and defers it there is a self-inconsistency the ADR does not reconcile.

cited_authority.ref: ADR-003 §2.5 property 1 ('the run's ReasoningSession') + §2.6 ('A sub-run is a run ... with the composing agent as orchestrator of its sub-run') vs §2.7's own rule that run-lifecycle-authorship widening arrives as an additive assignment under the owning record's amendment process

findings.locus: §2.3 Governance anchors to the graph — 'no routing choice can bypass a gate'
findings.claim: §2.3 asserts, as the load-bearing invariant that makes route freedom safe, that checkpoint gates 'anchor to checkpoints in the run and the graph, not to positions in a call graph; no routing choice can bypass a gate.' §2.2 makes direct agent-to-agent communication first-class, and §2.8 commits that 'opening a direct agent-to-agent route requires no ratification act; the §2.3 invariants are the entire control.' The record supplies no mechanism internal to itself — and cites none upstream — by which a gate anchored to a graph checkpoint binds a direct interaction that touches no graph checkpoint. A direct agent-to-agent interaction performing no graph write reaches neither the sole-owner gateway (ADR-002 §2.5) nor a graph-anchored gate. The unbypassability guarantee is therefore unsubstantiated for exactly the direct-route, no-graph-write interaction this record makes first-class and un-permissioned — and that guarantee is the whole basis of §3's Rationale that route freedom carries no governance loss. A load-bearing platform-principle claim resting on an unstated mechanism is a posture defect at this altitude: the record should stand only if the guarantee it leans on holds in the shape stated.

cited_authority.ref: ADR-003 §2.3 ('no routing choice can bypass a gate') unsubstantiated against §2.2 (first-class direct agent-to-agent routes) + §2.8 ('opening a direct agent-to-agent route requires no ratification act; the §2.3 invariants are the entire control'); gates anchor to graph checkpoints, a direct no-write route reaches none

findings.locus: §2.4 body vs §7 Cross-References — DDR-003 routing attribution
findings.claim: §2.4 body reads 'predicate-evaluation semantics are routed to the forthcoming constraint-validator service design (per DDR-002 §3's obligation-closure assignment; SDD-001 §4.2 names the constraint-validator as the single source of that predicate-evaluation)' — correctly grounded. But §7 Cross-References still reads: 'DDR-003 (Feedback Loop Governance) — governance policy for the promotion path; the routing of the Condition predicate vocabulary (the predicate grammar for scope conditions) onward to the forthcoming constraint-validator service design.' DDR-003 §9 routes only the promotion-scoped 'Condition predicate vocabulary — the predicate grammar for scope conditions … routed onward to the constraint-validator service design (forthcoming)'. §2.4 governs enterprise standards/directives/policy binding agents from the Standards plane — a broader object than the promotion Conditions DDR-003 scopes. The two §7 characterizations diverge from the §2.4 body's own corrected attribution: §7 leaves DDR-003 named as the routing source for predicate-evaluation the body correctly assigns to DDR-002 §3 / SDD-001 §4.2.

cited_authority.ref: ADR-003 §7 (DDR-003 entry, condition-predicate-vocabulary routing) vs §2.4 body ('per DDR-002 §3's obligation-closure assignment; SDD-001 §4.2 … single source') and DDR-003 §9 (Condition predicate vocabulary, promotion-scoped, narrower than §2.4's Standards-plane constraint binding)

findings.authority_locus: ADR-003 §2.4 body's own corrected attribution (DDR-002 §3 obligation-closure / SDD-001 §4.2 single-source) + DDR-003 §9 (Condition predicate vocabulary, promotion-scoped)

findings.locus: §2.6 recursion clause ('A sub-run is a run: the §2.5 anatomy invariants apply at every level') vs §2.5 property 1 and §2.7
findings.claim: §2.6 states 'A sub-run is a run: the §2.5 anatomy invariants apply at every level,' with 'the composing agent as orchestrator of its sub-run.' §2.5 property 1 requires for every run 'the run's ReasoningSession and its captured reasoning (ADR-001 §2.2; ADR-002 §2.6). No run is unrecorded.' ADR-002 §2.6 assigns 'the Agent Orchestration Engine (AOE) … owns the ReasoningSession lifecycle only'; DDR-002 §4 fixes ReasoningSession as AOE-owned ('Lifecycle owned by AOE') with no sub-session or nested-session shape. A sub-run whose composing agent (not AOE) acts as orchestrator would require either a ReasoningSession owned by a non-AOE component or a session-nesting shape the schema does not carry. §2.7 itself states that widening run-lifecycle authorship beyond the AOE 'arrives as a named, additive authority assignment under the owning record's own amendment process.' The recursion clause commits sub-runs-carry-the-ReasoningSession-invariant now, ahead of the very upstream amendment §2.7 declares the required path — a position the record's own §2.7 defers.

cited_authority.ref: ADR-003 §2.5 property 1 ('the run's ReasoningSession') + §2.6 ('A sub-run is a run … with the composing agent as orchestrator of its sub-run') vs ADR-002 §2.6 ('AOE … owns the ReasoningSession lifecycle only'), DDR-002 §4 (ReasoningSession lifecycle owned by AOE, no nested-session shape), and ADR-003 §2.7's own amendment-first rule

findings.locus: §2.6 four-class taxonomy — canon-named instances ASA, knowledge-service, AOE against their establishing authorities
findings.claim: Checked each canon-named component instance in §2.6 against its establishing authority in scope. ASA classed as a specialized (reasoning-bearing) agent conforms to ADR-002 §2.6 ('the Architecture Solutioning Agent (ASA) … is the authorized author of ReasoningProgress') and DDR-002 §4 (ASA authors ReasoningProgress). knowledge-service classed as the canonical substrate service (executes/enforces without authoring) conforms to ADR-002 §2.5 (sole-owner gateway), §2.6 (gateway 'is never itself the authoring authority'), and SDD-001 §1 ('sole executor of every authoritative write and the author of none'). AOE classed as the orchestrator owning run lifecycle conforms to ADR-002 §2.6 ('AOE … owns the ReasoningSession lifecycle only') and SDD-001 §3.4.1. Each named instance's class assignment resolves against its cited authority — the taxonomy names only components existing canon establishes for these three, as §2.6 claims. The attack that a named instance was misclassed or unestablished failed for all three (the model-access gateway instance is faulted separately).

cited_authority.ref: ADR-002 §2.5/§2.6 (author/executor/authorizer; ASA/AOE assignments) + SDD-001 §1/§3.4.1 + DDR-002 §4 — each of ASA, knowledge-service, AOE established and correctly classed
